analysis:
- Common high-performing template: Pareto prefilter → cheap per-point proxy (axis-aligned box volume ± scalarized weights) → adaptive shortlist (k‑proportional) → cached singleton HVs → CELF-style lazy greedy → bounded 1‑for‑1 swap cleanup. Top heuristics tune shortlist size, weight-count, and swap budget for better quality.
- Effective optimizations seen: singleton HV caching, selective pairwise HV cache when shortlist small, lexicographic deterministic tie‑breaking, lazy timestamps to avoid recomputing marginal gains, and cheap upper‑bounds (box_vol) to prune candidates.
- Performance/cost tradeoffs: larger shortlists and many weight vectors (Heur.10) improve approximation but blow up memory/HV calls; aggressive swap budgets improve HV but add expensive full-HV recomputations.
- Repeated inefficiencies across variants: O(N^2) Python loops for nondominated filtering and dominance checks, frequent np.vstack and full-set HV recomputations, list membership checks (cand in selected_idx) instead of set lookups, and occasional numeric/caching bugs (e.g., accumulating singleton gains instead of re-evaluating true HV in some paths).
- Correctness pitfalls: some heuristics assume additivity of singleton gains or increment hv by cached deltas (can be wrong); careful recomputation or provably-correct incremental HV updates are required.
- Practical knobs that matter most: shortlist selection rule (quality vs size), accuracy of HVC proxy (weight count and normalization), extent of lazy evaluation, and a conservative but effective swap budget.
experience: Prioritize (1) a strong deterministic shortlist: blend normalized rect_vol and scalarized scores, (2) precompute singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with timestamping and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit swaps to top candidates and update sets with O(1) structures, and (5) replace Python O(N^2) dominance checks / hot loops with vectorized or compiled routines and parallel HV evaluations.
current_reflection: Prioritize (1) a strong deterministic shortlist: blend normalized rect_vol and scalarized scores, (2) precompute singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with timestamping and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit swaps to top candidates and update sets with O(1) structures, and (5) replace Python O(N^2) dominance checks / hot loops with vectorized or compiled routines and parallel HV evaluations.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Keep Pareto prefilter, rank by cheap proxies, run CELF with cached singleton HVs, cap pairwise caching to ≤40, size shortlist ≈ c·k (adaptive with Pareto size), prune with fast upper bounds, update hv_selected incrementally, and bound swap iterations. Vectorize dominance/score steps and avoid huge weight sets—use few weights for ranking then exact HV on shortlist.

Prefer a k‑proportional deterministic shortlist, CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and pairwise when small), tight upper‑bound pruning, and a bounded incremental swap pass. Reuse vectorized proxies and avoid full HV recomputations inside loops. Validate incremental updates (HV is not additive). Tune weight/shortlist sizes to HV‑call budget; measure HV calls and iterate.

Make shortlists adaptive (upper-bound marginal gain → size), use CELF with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, and prune candidates by cheap upper bounds before exact HV. Implement incremental HV updates or fast specialized HV routines, batch/parallel HV evaluations, deterministic tie-breaks, and small bounded local swap budgets. Diversify a few scalarization weights, but avoid huge weight sets unless approximation budgets are explicit.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: deterministic shortlist, normalized rect_vol, scalarized scores, singleton/pairwise HV cache, CELF lazy greedy (timestamping), tight upper‑bound pruning, vectorized/compiled dominance, batched/parallel HV, bounded local swaps.
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points; build seeded deterministic shortlist by combining normalized rect_vol and a few scalarizations; precompute singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs (cap size); run CELF with timestamps and cheap upper bounds; batch/parallel HV evaluations; vectorize dominance checks; limit swaps to top candidates with O(1) update sets; ensure k.
- Avoid: O(N^2) Python loops and nested HV recomputations, huge weight sets, unbounded swap iterations, nondeterministic tie‑breaking.
- Explanation: Cuts HV calls and memory, speeds hot loops via vectorized/compiled routines, preserves quality with cached incremental HV deltas and selective pairwise info, and yields reproducible, bounded k-sized outputs.
